title: Spotting Mode Collapse
concept: The practical way to detect mode collapse is to generate a large batch of samples and visually inspect them for suspicious repetition or lack of variety — a visual diagnostic, not a purely numeric one, since loss curves can look healthy even during collapse.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does the generator have no built-in pull toward covering the full diversity of real data?

options.text: Because it's only ever rewarded for fooling the discriminator on the current batch — not for variety
Because the discriminator explicitly forbids variety
Because diversity is mathematically impossible for generators

exercises.explain: Right — no reward, no penalty for narrowness, so it can quietly settle on one winning output. Today: how to actually catch that happening.

cards.title: So... how would you even notice?
cards.body: If mode collapse doesn't show up as an obvious crash or an error message, how would you catch it while training your own GAN? The answer is refreshingly low-tech: look at the pictures.

cards.title: The batch-and-inspect method
cards.body: The standard way to spot mode collapse: generate a large batch of samples (say, 64 or 100) all at once, and lay them out in a grid. Then look — is there real variety, or does the same handful of outputs keep showing up?

cards.title: A detective's lineup
cards.body: It's the same trick a detective uses with a police lineup: looking at one photo at a time, a repeated face is easy to miss. Line up dozens of photos side by side, and a suspiciously repeated face jumps out immediately — same jaw, same eyes, same everything, just wearing a different hat.

cards.title: The 8x8 grid test
cards.body: Generate 64 samples and arrange them in an 8x8 grid. If a healthy generator trained on 10 digit styles is working, you'd expect to see a rough mix of digits scattered across the grid. If you instead spot the same 2-3 digit shapes repeating dozens of times, that's your visual red flag for mode collapse.

cards.title: "If the loss numbers look fine, training must be fine"
cards.body: This is the trap: a collapsed generator's loss can look perfectly healthy, even LOW — because it's very good at fooling the discriminator with its one winning trick. A clean-looking loss curve does NOT rule out mode collapse. That's exactly why this is a visual check, not a purely numeric one.

cards.title: Numeric check vs. visual check
cards.body: Two ways to monitor training — and why only one catches this:

left.text:
Can look healthy
Even during collapse
Misses the pattern

right.text:
Shows real variety (or not)
Repeats jump out visually
Catches what numbers miss

exercises.prompt: What's the standard practical method for spotting mode collapse?

options.text: Generate a large batch of samples, lay them out in a grid, and visually check for repetition
Check only whether the final training loss number is below some threshold
Count how many total training steps have completed
Check the size of the model file on disk

exercises.explain: Generating a big batch and eyeballing it for repeated patterns is the go-to way to catch mode collapse.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A generator can have a low, healthy-looking loss value and still be severely mode-collapsed.
exercises.explain: True — that's the trap. A collapsed generator can be VERY good at fooling the discriminator with its one narrow trick, which keeps the loss looking fine while diversity is missing.

exercises.sentence: Because loss numbers alone can look fine during collapse, detecting it usually relies on a ___ check of a large batch of samples, not just the numbers.
exercises.explain: You have to actually look at a batch of samples laid out together — repetition is something you SEE, not something a single loss number reveals.

exercises.prompt: You generate a 64-sample grid from your GAN to check for mode collapse. Which observation would be the clearest red flag?

options.text: Roughly 50 of the 64 samples look like near-identical variations of the same output
The 64 samples show a wide, roughly even mix of different outputs
The training loss curve is smooth and steadily decreasing

exercises.explain: 50-of-64 near-identical samples is exactly the repetition pattern a visual grid check is designed to catch — a smooth loss curve alone wouldn't reveal it.

exercises.prompt: You've confirmed mode collapse is happening by eyeballing a sample grid full of repeats. What's the natural next question?

options.text: Are there any known techniques that help reduce the chances of mode collapse happening in the first place?
How do you make the images higher resolution?
How do you speed up the discriminator's training?
How do you convert the images to grayscale?

exercises.explain: Exactly! Next: a quick tour of some of the partial fixes researchers have developed for mode collapse.
